summary: I was born in Hiroshima Ken on January 9, 1898, and spent my early years there until I was thirteen. I have many memories of playing in the mountains and fishing in the rivers around Hiroshima Prefecture. My family was deeply rooted in Shin Buddhism, and we lived next to a Buddhist temple, which was an important part of our community.

After coming to the United States at age thirteen, I pursued my higher education at the University of Puget Sound, which is now located in Tacoma. I majored in philosophy there, as I was very interested in philosophy and theology. This college education was part of my journey to becoming a theologian in the U.S.

My father was farming near Tacoma when I arrived in the U.S. at age thirteen, and I helped him on the farm before attending school. Tacoma was where my family settled, and it was a place where many Japanese immigrants lived under difficult conditions. The Japanese community there faced discrimination, but it was also where I began my education and life in America.

I attended the Pacific School of Religion in Berkeley, California, where I earned my Bachelor of Divinity and Master of Arts degrees. My studies there focused mainly on theology, complementing my earlier philosophy major. This seminary education was crucial for my work as a religious leader and for understanding the challenges faced by Japanese Americans.

Berkeley, California, is where I attended seminary at the Pacific School of Religion. It was an important place for my theological education, where I deepened my understanding of religion and philosophy. Berkeley represented a center of learning that shaped my future role in the community.

My family practiced Shin Buddhism, and we were very pious Buddhists living next to a Buddhist temple with which we had a special relationship. This religious background was a significant part of my upbringing in Hiroshima Prefecture. Although I later studied Christianity in the U.S., my roots in Shin Buddhism remained important to me.

After the war, I often visited Brawley in the Imperial Valley, California, where many Japanese farmers lived in conditions similar to those I remembered from my early days in America. The Japanese community there faced hardships, moving their houses with horses when their land leases expired. These visits reminded me of the struggles Japanese immigrants endured in places like Tacoma and Seattle long ago.

The Imperial Valley in California was home to many Japanese farmers after the war, including those in Brawley whom I visited frequently. The farming community there lived under tough conditions, often moving their homes when leases expired. This area reflected the ongoing challenges faced by Japanese immigrants in California, similar to what I experienced in Washington state decades earlier.

California was a significant place in the history of Japanese immigrants, including myself. The anti-Japanese sentiment was strong there, with narrow, poor housing conditions for Japanese families, much like what I saw in Tacoma and Seattle. After the war, many Japanese farmers in California, especially in places like Brawley and the Imperial Valley, continued to face difficult living and working conditions.

The Gentlemen's Agreement of 1907 was a turning point for Japanese immigration to the U.S., and I came here just before the yobiyose immigration was ended by revisions to this agreement. After the agreement ended, immigration was banned, and anti-Japanese exclusion movements intensified. This agreement and its aftermath shaped the lives of many Japanese immigrants, including my own family’s decision to bring me over at age thirteen.
